title: Technical Specialist / Engineer
audience: Engineering, platform, integration, data, and cyber roles.

questions.text: When designing a system that uses AI, what is "technical debt" typically referring to?
options: Deferred licensing and platform costs created during AI implementation planning when building production AI systems.
Design or implementation choices that are expedient in the short term but create future rework or quality issues.
Formal finance arrangements used to fund experimental AI research programs for secure and scalable AI services.
The training process used to tune model behaviour before production use when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.

questions.text: What is the role of a "Proof of Concept" (PoC) in an AI project's design phase?
options: To release the complete AI solution to every production user immediately during model deployment and operations.
To demonstrate the feasibility and potential of a specific AI idea or technology on a small scale.
To produce the full operating documentation before design decisions are tested when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
To obtain full program funding before validating technical and business feasibility.

questions.text: In AI system design, what is "scalability"?
options: A measurement process for comparing the size of different deployed AI models within the same production engineering context.
The capability of an AI system to handle increasing workloads or user demand effectively.
A process for checking AI models and endpoints for security vulnerabilities.
The ease of creating visual screens and diagrams for an AI-enabled service.

questions.text: What is a "monolithic architecture" versus a "microservices architecture" in the context of AI application deployment?
options: Monolithic architectures are always preferred because AI services require one application when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
Monolithic means a single, tightly coupled application; microservices means breaking it into smaller, independent services.
Monolithic systems use earlier model types while microservices use newer model types during model deployment and operations.
Monolithic deployment is limited to on-premise systems while microservices require cloud for secure and scalable AI services.

questions.text: When designing an AI solution, what is "interoperability"?
options: The ability of an AI system to operate only with one vendor ecosystem when building production AI systems.
The capability of different AI components or systems to work together and exchange information effectively.
The response speed of an AI model when handling user requests at runtime for secure and scalable AI services.
The process of designing a model's internal algorithmic approach when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.

questions.text: Which of these is a key consideration for a solution architect integrating a third-party AI service?
options: The visual branding and interface style of the selected AI service provider.
Data privacy, security, API documentation, and service reliability.
The personal preferences and working habits of the vendor development team.
The size of the vendor engineering team assigned to the AI service.

questions.text: What is a "design pattern" in software engineering, and why might it be relevant for AI solution design?
options: A specific algorithm used to generate designs or user-interface layouts for secure and scalable AI services.
A reusable solution to commonly occurring problems in software design, which can offer proven AI integration strategies.
A visual diagramming tool for drafting high-level AI model concepts when building production AI systems.
A mandatory documentation template applied to every AI project regardless of context during model deployment and operations.

questions.text: When designing an AI application, what is a "data contract" (or data schema agreement) between systems?
options: A legal agreement governing data sharing between two companies when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
A formal agreement defining the structure, format, and meaning of data exchanged between different components or services.
A security control for encrypting AI data while it moves between systems during model deployment and operations within the same production engineering context.
A document describing the visual layout of a database or reporting screen for secure and scalable AI services.

questions.text: What is the difference between "model inference" and "model training" from an architectural perspective?
options: Inference always uses GPUs while training always relies on CPUs when building production AI systems.
Inference is using a trained model to make predictions; training is the process of teaching the model from data. These often have different hardware/scalability needs.
Inference is used only for small models while training is reserved for large models for secure and scalable AI services.
Inference takes place before deployment while training only happens after release when integrating systems in an enterprise stack within the same production engineering context.

questions.text: In the context of AI governance design, what is a "model card"?
options: A payment card used to fund AI services and cloud model usage during model deployment and operations.
A short document accompanying an AI model, providing details on its performance, usage, limitations, and ethical considerations.
A physical inventory card showing the deployed model version number when integrating systems in an enterprise stack within the same production engineering context.
A storage component that holds model weights for later deployment when building production AI systems.

questions.text: When designing an AI deployment strategy, what is the role of a "feature store"?
options: A marketplace where teams can publish, purchase, and reuse AI models for secure and scalable AI services.
A centralized system for managing, serving, and sharing engineered features consistently across model training and inference.
A database dedicated to storing raw data before feature engineering when building production AI systems.
A reporting tool used to visualise which features influenced predictions during model deployment and operations within the same production engineering context.

questions.text: What does "container orchestration" (e.g., Kubernetes) provide for deploying AI models?
options: A scoring method for arranging AI-generated music into production tracks when integrating systems in an enterprise stack within the same production engineering context.
Automation for deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications (including AI models) across clusters of machines.
A security method for encrypting data stored inside container images during model deployment and operations.
A design tool for building graphical user interfaces around AI services for secure and scalable AI services.

questions.text: In the design of an AI-powered product, what is a "feedback loop"?
options: A recurring software defect that appears repeatedly in the AI application code when building production AI systems.
A mechanism to capture user interactions or real-world outcomes to continuously improve the AI model over time.
A communication layer that lets multiple AI models exchange messages for secure and scalable AI services.
A workflow component that causes a model to repeat a task until stopped when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.

questions.text: What is "data lineage" and why is it important in complex AI data architectures?
options: Tracking the organisational history of the developers who built the AI model during model deployment and operations within the same production engineering context.
Tracing the flow of data from its source through various transformations to its use in AI models, crucial for data governance and debugging.
A performance optimisation method for reducing model training time when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
A classification method for grouping datasets by type, owner, or domain when building production AI systems.

questions.text: Which architectural pattern is often preferred for highly scalable and responsive AI inference services?
options: Nightly batch processing where predictions are produced after daily jobs finish.
Real-time, event-driven microservices with serverless functions or containerized endpoints.
A single monolithic application deployed to one fixed on-premise server when building production AI systems.
Manual release and scaling work performed by operations teams on request during model deployment and operations.

questions.text: Why are "adversarial examples" a significant concern for security architects when designing AI systems?
options: They reduce runtime performance by forcing models to process more complex inputs when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
They are subtly manipulated inputs that cause AI models to make incorrect predictions, posing security risks in critical systems.
They describe AI systems that deliberately become hostile toward end users during model deployment and operations.
They are ordinary training errors caused by incomplete or poor-quality datasets for secure and scalable AI services.

questions.text: When designing an AI system, what is the significance of a "service mesh" like Istio or Linkerd?
options: To manage model weight files during distributed deep learning training when building production AI systems.
To provide advanced traffic management, observability, and security features for microservices, including AI endpoints.
To create a dedicated network where AI models communicate directly for secure and scalable AI services.
To package and deploy AI models to edge devices with less overhead when integrating systems in an enterprise stack within the same production engineering context.

questions.text: In the context of data pipelines for AI, what is a "data lakehouse" architecture?
options: A specialised database intended only for storing AI model artefacts during model deployment and operations.
A hybrid data architecture that combines the flexibility of data lakes with the data management features of data warehouses.
A streaming platform for moving IoT data into analytics systems in real time when integrating systems in an enterprise stack.
A graphical interface for exploring and visualising large datasets when building production AI systems.

questions.text: What is "model quantization" in AI deployment?
options: Splitting a large AI model into smaller components for easier deployment for secure and scalable AI services.
A technique to reduce the precision of numerical representations (e.g., from 32-bit to 8-bit floats) in AI models to optimize for inference speed and memory.
A security method for preventing unauthorised access to deployed AI models when building production AI systems within the same production engineering context.
Adding extra layers to a neural network to increase model capacity during model deployment and operations.

questions.text: Which of these is a core responsibility of an Enterprise Architect in driving AI adoption across an organization?
options: Building separate AI models directly for each individual business unit when integrating systems in an enterprise stack within the same production engineering context.
Defining AI strategy, establishing architectural principles, identifying common platforms, and ensuring alignment with overall enterprise objectives.
Manually cleaning every dataset before it is used in model training during model deployment and operations.
Personally approving and managing every budget for individual AI projects for secure and scalable AI services.
